---
title: "Project Management and Coding Principles"
engine: julia
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
  message: false
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
---

## Motivation and Learning Goals

::: {.callout-important title="Motivation" appearance="simple"}
- LLMs can generate a large amount of code in seconds.
- Without a disciplined project structure, the generated code can quickly become difficult to understand, validate, and reproduce.
:::

::: {.callout-important title="Learning Goals" appearance="simple"}
- Learn how to organize a computational research project.
- Use simple tools to make the workflow reproducible.
- Apply coding principles that make code easier for humans and AI agents to review.
:::

## Directory Structure

### One Example

- The following tree shows my default structure for a computational research project.
- The Git repository stores source code, documentation, and other files that benefit from version control.
- External storage holds large data files and generated results.

```text
git-repository/
├── src/
├── scripts/
├── memory/
├── validation/
├── reports/
├── writeup/
├── README.md
├── Makefile
├── data/ -> Dropbox/project/data/
└── results/ -> Dropbox/project/results/

Dropbox/project/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   └── processed/
└── results/
```

### Purpose of Each Directory

- **`src/`**
  - Stores reusable functions, types, and modules.
  - Example files include `blp_estimator.jl` and `solve_dynamic_game.jl`.
- **`scripts/`**
  - Stores entry-point programs that perform a specific task and may create output.
  - Example files include:
    - `clean_dataset_a.jl`;
    - `merge_datasets.jl`; and
    - `estimate_blp_demand.jl`.
- **`memory/`**
  - Stores durable project decisions, workflow notes, and implementation context.
- **`validation/`**
  - Stores code and QMD documents that check numerical or empirical results.
- **`reports/`**
  - Stores QMD documents used to share results with coauthors.
- **`writeup/`**
  - Stores paper drafts and presentation slides.
- **`README.md`**
  - Explains the project structure, environment setup, and canonical commands.
  - A clear README helps both collaborators and AI agents navigate the project.
- **`data/` and `results/`**
  - Point to large files kept outside the Git repository in this example.
  - Raw data, processed data, and generated results have distinct locations.

::: {.callout-tip title="Scripts versus src" appearance="simple"}
- How should we decide whether a function belongs in src or scripts?
- Put a function in src if it implements core project logic or is used by two or more scripts.
- Keep other functions in scripts, close to the workflow that uses them.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="This is one possible structure" appearance="simple"}
- The exact directory names are less important than clear and stable distinctions.
- On macOS, I use symbolic links to connect the data and results directories in the repository to folders stored in Dropbox.
- This setup lets my code and AI coding agents follow the same project-relative paths from the repository root, making it easier to work with files stored outside the Git repository.
- I have not tested this workflow on Windows, where symbolic links may require a different setup or behave differently.
- Avoid hard-coded absolute paths. Build paths from the project root with tools such as `joinpath`.
:::

## Makefiles

- A Makefile records commands under short target names and defines the order in which tasks should run.
- Ideally, one command should reproduce all figures and tables in a project.
  - Gentzkow and Shapiro emphasize this goal in their guide to research code and data.
- A rule can contain:
  - a **target**, which names the task;
  - **prerequisites**, which must run first; and
  - a **recipe**, which contains the commands.
- The following example defines a small empirical workflow.

```makefile
.PHONY: all clean merge descriptive reduced_form

all: descriptive reduced_form

clean:
	julia --project=. scripts/clean_dataset_a.jl
	julia --project=. scripts/clean_dataset_b.jl

merge: clean
	julia --project=. scripts/merge_datasets.jl

descriptive: merge
	julia --project=. scripts/generate_descriptive_statistics.jl

reduced_form: merge
	julia --project=. scripts/estimate_reduced_form_1.jl
	julia --project=. scripts/estimate_reduced_form_2.jl
```

- `make all` runs the complete workflow.
- `make descriptive` runs data cleaning, data merging, and descriptive-statistics production.
- `make reduced_form` runs data cleaning, data merging, and the two reduced-form estimations.
- Each Makefile recipe line begins with a tab, not spaces.
- Windows does not include `make` by default, so Windows users need an environment such as WSL or Git Bash with Make installed.
- If the README or `AGENTS.md` identifies these targets as the canonical commands, an AI agent can use the same workflow as a human collaborator.

## Coding Principles

- AI-generated code still needs to be readable because readability makes mistakes easier to find.
- The following principles are adapted from *The Art of Readable Code* for economics research and AI-assisted programming.

### Names and Comments

#### Use Clear and Accurate Names

- A name should contain enough information to distinguish its role, but length alone does not make it clearer.
  - Unclear: `df`, `temp`, `matrix_for_regressors`
  - Too long: `individual_level_purchase_history_data`
  - Clear in context: `purchase_history`, `regressor_matrix`
- Short conventional names are acceptable when their meaning and scope are clear.
  - `X` and `y` can be appropriate inside a short regression function.
  - `i` and `j` can be appropriate in a short mathematical loop.
- Use domain-specific iterator names when the indices have distinct meanings.

```julia
# Unclear
for i in axes(transition_matrix, 1), j in axes(transition_matrix, 2)
    update_probability!(transition_matrix, i, j)
end

# Clear
for current_state in axes(transition_matrix, 1),
    next_state in axes(transition_matrix, 2)
    update_probability!(transition_matrix, current_state, next_state)
end
```

- Use exact words.
  - Prefer `maximum_iterations` to `iteration_limit_number`.
  - Prefer a boolean name such as `is_child` or `is_under_twenty` to an ambiguous noun such as `child` or `twenty`.

#### Avoid Redundant Comments

- Do not use a comment only to restate an obvious line of code.

```julia
# Add one to the iteration counter.
iteration += 1
```

- Use comments to explain information that the code cannot express directly.
  - Explain the economic or numerical reason for a step.
  - Record a non-obvious constraint or design choice.
  - Summarize the purpose of a complicated block.

```julia
# Keep the same simulation draws across specifications so that differences
# reflect the specification rather than Monte Carlo noise.
rng = MersenneTwister(base_seed)
```

### Control Flow and Intermediate Objects

#### Make Control Flow Easy to Read

- Put the changing value on the left and the stable reference value on the right.
  - Prefer `iteration >= maximum_iterations`.
- Express conditions positively when possible.
  - Prefer `is_valid_sample` to `!is_invalid_sample`.
- Return early when an invalid case does not need the main logic.
- Reduce nesting so that the main path remains visible.

```julia
# Harder to scan
function estimate_if_valid(data)
    if is_valid_sample(data)
        estimate = estimate_model(data)
        return estimate
    else
        return nothing
    end
end

# Easier to scan
function estimate_if_valid(data)
    is_valid_sample(data) || return nothing
    return estimate_model(data)
end
```

#### Use Intermediate Objects Deliberately

- Give a complicated condition a meaningful name when the name explains the logic.

```julia
has_enough_observations = nrow(data) >= minimum_observations
has_finite_outcomes = all(isfinite, data.outcome)

if has_enough_observations && has_finite_outcomes
    estimate_model(data)
end
```

- Do not introduce a temporary object that merely renames a simple expression used once.
- Define a variable close to where it is used and keep its scope as small as practical.
- Avoid mutable global variables in computational code.

### Refactoring

#### Give Each Function One Clear Purpose

- Suppose a text file contains one temperature per line.
- A single function can mix file input, calculation, and reporting.

```julia
function summarize_temperatures(path)
    temperatures = parse.(Float64, readlines(path))
    average_temperature = sum(temperatures) / length(temperatures)
    println("Average temperature: $(round(average_temperature, digits=1)) °C")
    return average_temperature
end
```

- Separate these responsibilities when each one represents a meaningful operation.

```julia
function read_temperatures(path)
    return parse.(Float64, readlines(path))
end

function calculate_average(temperatures)
    return sum(temperatures) / length(temperatures)
end

function print_temperature_summary(average_temperature)
    println("Average temperature: $(round(average_temperature, digits=1)) °C")
end

function summarize_temperatures(path)
    temperatures = read_temperatures(path)
    average_temperature = calculate_average(temperatures)
    print_temperature_summary(average_temperature)
    return average_temperature
end
```

- In this example:
  - `read_temperatures` handles file input;
  - `calculate_average` performs the calculation;
  - `print_temperature_summary` formats and displays the result; and
  - `summarize_temperatures` coordinates the complete workflow.
- Repeated nontrivial logic is a strong signal that it should become a function.
- A function can also be useful when it is called only once if it isolates a meaningful step and makes the workflow easier to inspect.

## Additional Tools for Working Across Computers

### Dotfiles and chezmoi

- Dotfiles are configuration files for shells, Git, editors, and other command-line tools.
- Track only configuration files that you have inspected.
  - Do not commit access tokens, credentials, session files, or other secrets.
  - Do not automatically track an entire configuration directory such as `.codex/` or `.claude/`.
- [chezmoi](https://www.chezmoi.io/) manages selected dotfiles across multiple computers.
  - It stores a source state that can be tracked with Git.
  - `chezmoi diff` previews the changes that would be made on the current computer.
  - `chezmoi apply` updates the selected files.
  - Templates can represent differences across macOS, Linux, and Windows.

### Tailscale and SSH

- [Tailscale](https://tailscale.com/docs/features/tailscale-ssh) creates a private network between computers.
- Tailscale SSH can authenticate and authorize SSH connections according to the tailnet's access-control policies.
- I use this type of setup to connect my laptop to another computer at home.
- SSH determines where commands run.
  - After connecting to a remote computer, commands execute in the remote shell.
  - An AI coding CLI can work on the remote computer only if it is installed, authenticated, and permitted to access the relevant files and commands there.
  - Opening an SSH connection alone does not give an AI tool additional access.

### Shell Shortcuts

- Shell aliases or functions can shorten commands that you run repeatedly.
- For example, a shortcut can:
  - move to a frequently used project directory; or
  - connect to a named SSH host.

```bash
alias cd-cluster-project='cd /path/to/cluster/project'
alias ssh-home='ssh home-desktop'
```

- These shortcuts save time, but their definitions differ across zsh, Bash, and PowerShell.

## References

- [Gentzkow and Shapiro, *Code and Data for the Social Sciences: A Practitioner's Guide*](https://web.stanford.edu/~gentzkow/research/CodeAndData.pdf)
- Boswell and Foucher, *The Art of Readable Code*
- [GNU Make Manual](https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/make.html)
- [chezmoi documentation](https://www.chezmoi.io/)
- [Tailscale SSH documentation](https://tailscale.com/docs/features/tailscale-ssh)
